ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Gulai Kawah; mapped to the nearest parent class 'curry dish' (FOODON_03315595). A more specific Malaysian meat curry class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based curry dish — Southeast Asian style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Gulai Kawah. Code A0FJP (meat-based curry, Southeast Asian style) is the closest defensible anchor. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Gulai Kawah or a sufficiently close Malaysian meat curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Gulai Kawah is approximately 250 g, representing a generous portion of slow-cooked meat in thick spiced coconut-milk gravy, typically served alongside white rice or Nasi Minyak at communal meals or festive gatherings.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Meat Curry / Gulai Daging entries; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef and coconut-milk component data
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — MyFCD; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single MyFCD entry for 'Gulai Kawah' by name. Values are adapted from the closest MyFCD gulai daging (beef curry) entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for beef, coconut milk, and kerisik. The input record cited HPB Singapore; HPB does not publish a specific Gulai Kawah entry. IMR MyFCD is the primary approved source for Malaysian dishes. Saturated fat estimated from coconut-milk and beef fat fractions using USDA SR Legacy data. All values are consistent with the input record's per_100g figures and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on beef curry with coconut milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gulai Kawah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 (low) is estimated by analogy with meat-based coconut-milk curries, which are consistently reported in the low-GI range (35–45) due to high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption. Kerisik (toasted coconut) adds additional fibre. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 18.75) / 100 = 7.5. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 7.5 × 2.5 = 18.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gulai Kawah to occasional consumption, reduce added salt during preparation, use reduced-fat coconut milk, and keep portion size to no more than 150 g to limit sodium intake to under 500 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 40) offset by high saturated fat from coconut milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and high protein content of Gulai Kawah support moderate postprandial glucose control, but the high saturated fat load (approximately 9.8 g/100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a controlled portion (150–200 g) paired with a non-starchy vegetable side and monitor total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~9.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk and fatty meat cuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Gulai Kawah is high in saturated fat, which is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g) on an infrequent basis, choose leaner cuts of beef or mutton, and substitute reduced-fat coconut milk to lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, galangal) present, but high saturated fat may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and galangal in the spice paste provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals that may benefit rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high saturated fat content from coconut milk may counteract these benefits, so moderate portion sizes and lean meat cuts are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gulai Kawah is a traditional Malay slow-cooked meat curry originating from the East Coast states of Kelantan and Terengganu in Malaysia. The name 'Kawah' refers to the massive, deep cast-iron woks (kawah) traditionally used to cook this dish over an open fire during large community gatherings, weddings (kenduri), and religious festivals. The dish features chunks of beef or mutton slow-simmered in a thick, rich, and deeply aromatic coconut-milk gravy. The spice paste (rempah) is complex, incorporating dried chilies, shallots, garlic, ginger, galangal, lemongrass, star anise, cinnamon, and cloves, along with kerisik (toasted grated coconut) that thickens the sauce and imparts a distinctive nutty depth. The extended slow-cooking process yields exceptionally tender meat and a concentrated, dark, intensely flavoured sauce. Per 100 g, Gulai Kawah provides approximately 225 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 16.2 g fat (of which approximately 9.8 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 7.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is moderate-to-high at 320 mg/100 g. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) due to its high protein and fat content slowing carbohydrate digestion. Clinically, it is high in saturated fat and sodium, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease; those with Type 2 Diabetes may consume it in controlled portions given its low GI. It is typically served with white rice or Nasi Minyak and is a centrepiece dish at Malay festive occasions.

confidence_reason: No exact MyFCD or HPB entry for 'Gulai Kawah' by name was found. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source gulai daging (beef curry) entries in IMR MyFCD and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. The input record's per_100g figures are consistent with these references and have been retained. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from coconut-milk and beef fat composition data. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable meat-based coconut-milk curries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.6 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for beef (lean and fat) and coconut milk in proportions consistent with a typical gulai kawah recipe; these values are not directly reported in a single approved-source entry for this dish. 2. cholesterol_mg and water_g are set to -1: detailed cholesterol and moisture data for the specific Gulai Kawah preparation are not available as a single entry in IMR MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. The input record cited HPB Singapore as the source; HPB does not publish a specific Gulai Kawah nutrient entry. The primary approved source for Malaysian dishes is IMR MyFCD, which has been used as the basis for adaptation. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gulai Kawah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry for Gulai Kawah exists in either system as of 2024. 6. This dish is inherently halal (beef or mutton with plant-based spices and coconut milk) and gluten-free. 7. Multilingual local names: this is a Malaysian Malay dish; Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable as primary local-language names for this cuisine category. The Malay name 'Gulai Kawah' is the canonical local name.
